statement: LiteLLM's core design principle is that every provider call should look identical to an OpenAI API call from the caller's perspective. It achieves this by translating the OpenAI request schema into each provider's native format and translating the response back. The practical consequence is that application code never needs to branch on provider — the provider is a configuration value, not a code path. This is the reason LiteLLM exists: it separates the 'what to call' decision from the 'how to call it' mechanics.

LiteLLM's router supports cost-based routing: given a list of model deployments, it can automatically select the cheapest deployment that satisfies latency and capability constraints. The underlying philosophy is that LLM calls should be commodity-priced, with the application specifying capability requirements (context length, function calling support) rather than specific models. This is why the Router's model_list accepts multiple entries for the same logical model across different providers.

AWS Bedrock, Google Vertex AI, and Azure OpenAI each have non-standard authentication flows that LiteLLM handles differently and inconsistently. Bedrock requires boto3-style credentials (AWS_ACCESS_KEY_ID + AWS_SECRET_ACCESS_KEY + AWS_REGION_NAME) but also silently falls back to EC2 instance role if env vars are absent, which causes local development to fail only on machines without AWS config. Vertex requires a service account JSON file path via GOOGLE_APPLICATION_CREDENTIALS, not a key string. Azure requires api_base and api_version in addition to api_key; omitting api_version returns a generic 404 that gives no hint about the missing parameter. These three providers account for the majority of auth-related LiteLLM GitHub issues.

LiteLLM's cost tracking (response.usage and litellm.completion_cost()) relies on a hardcoded price table that is updated with each release but lags provider pricing changes by days to weeks. Providers that do not return token counts in their response (some Bedrock models, some Cohere endpoints) cause LiteLLM to estimate token counts using tiktoken, which can be off by 10-30% for non-English text. Budget guardrails built on LiteLLM cost data should include a 30% safety margin and should be validated monthly against actual provider invoices.

LiteLLM operates in two distinct modes: SDK mode (import litellm; litellm.completion()) and Proxy mode (a standalone HTTP server that exposes an OpenAI-compatible REST API). SDK mode is appropriate for single-process applications where the developer controls the codebase. Proxy mode is appropriate for teams sharing LLM access, because it centralizes auth, rate limiting, cost tracking, and model routing without requiring each downstream application to install or configure LiteLLM. The proxy's config.yaml is the single source of truth for model aliases, fallbacks, and budgets in multi-team deployments.

LiteLLM releases new versions daily or near-daily, and the official documentation site often lags 1-3 versions behind the PyPI package. New provider support, parameter names, and router strategies introduced in recent releases may only be documented in GitHub release notes or the CHANGELOG, not on docs.litellm.ai. When a LiteLLM feature does not behave as the docs describe, checking the GitHub releases page for the installed version is the correct first debugging step before opening an issue.

LiteLLM's Router supports two distinct fallback mechanisms: model-level fallbacks (try a different deployment of the same logical model on failure) and request-level fallbacks (try a completely different model when all deployments of the primary model fail). These are configured separately via fallbacks and context_window_fallbacks in Router. context_window_fallbacks is specifically for handling 'context length exceeded' errors by automatically re-routing to a model with a larger window — this is the most frequently needed fallback in production and the one most commonly misconfigured as a generic fallback instead.

LiteLLM's response streaming from providers that do not natively support SSE (some Bedrock models, Cohere, AI21) is emulated by the SDK: it buffers the full response, then yields a single 'chunk' that contains the entire completion. Code that relies on streaming for latency improvements (displaying tokens as they arrive) will silently get the full-latency non-streaming behavior for these providers without any error or warning. The only way to detect this is to measure time-to-first-chunk: for true streaming it is ~300ms, for emulated streaming it matches completion latency. Teams building latency-sensitive UIs that need to support multiple providers must verify streaming support per-provider in the LiteLLM provider matrix and implement provider-appropriate UX for non-streaming providers.

LiteLLM's budget management (set via max_budget in Router or via the Proxy's budget config) enforces a hard cost ceiling per model group, per user, or per API key. When a budget is exceeded, LiteLLM raises a BudgetExceededError rather than routing to a fallback, because budget limits are intentional policy constraints not transient failures. This behavior surprises teams who configure budgets as a soft throttle and expect graceful degradation: if the primary model group exhausts its budget mid-day, all requests fail with BudgetExceededError even if other model groups have remaining budget. The correct architecture for budget-aware routing is to configure separate budget limits per tier in the model_list and use the router's fallbacks to cascade from premium to economy models when the premium tier's budget is exhausted, not to use a single global budget that kills all traffic.

LiteLLM's caching layer (Redis or in-memory) caches based on an exact hash of the request parameters including the full messages array, model name, and sampling parameters. Cache hits require byte-identical inputs, so any variation in message history, system prompt, or temperature produces a cache miss. This makes LiteLLM caching useful only for truly idempotent requests — batch processing the same input multiple times, semantic search embeddings with fixed queries, or deterministic classification calls. It is NOT useful for conversational AI where each turn appends a new message. A common misuse is enabling caching on a chat application expecting reduced costs, then observing 0% cache hit rate because no two requests in a conversation have identical message arrays. Semantic caching (approximate match via embeddings) is available as an experimental feature but requires additional infrastructure and introduces false positive risks.

LiteLLM supports custom callbacks via litellm.callbacks (list of callback objects) for logging, monitoring, and cost tracking integration. Callbacks receive success_handler() and failure_handler() calls with the full request/response data, making them the correct extension point for sending traces to Langfuse, Helicone, or custom databases. The key design constraint: callbacks run synchronously in the same thread as the completion call by default, adding their execution time to total request latency. For callbacks that perform I/O (HTTP requests to Langfuse, database writes), this can add 50-200ms per call. The solution is to use async callbacks (implement async def async_success_handler()) combined with an AsyncOpenAI-style client, which moves callback execution off the critical path. Synchronous callbacks are acceptable only for pure-computation tasks like local metrics counters.

LiteLLM's load balancing strategy 'least-busy' routes new requests to the deployment with the fewest in-flight requests. This strategy assumes all deployments have similar throughput — when mixed-capacity deployments exist (e.g., a gpt-4o deployment with 1M TPM quota alongside a gpt-4o-mini deployment with 5M TPM quota), least-busy overloads the smaller-capacity deployment because it has fewer in-flight requests at any moment but proportionally lower headroom. The result is systematic 429s on the higher-capacity model being underutilized. The correct strategy for mixed-capacity pools is 'usage-based-routing' with explicit rpm and tpm limits per deployment in the model_list, which routes proportionally to remaining quota rather than raw in-flight count. Misconfigured load balancing causes capacity waste that is invisible until a 429 wave triggers investigation.

LiteLLM Proxy's virtual keys (/key/generate endpoint) enable per-team, per-user, or per-application API key management with individual rate limits, budget caps, and model access controls. Virtual keys are the recommended architecture for multi-tenant LLM gateways: instead of distributing real provider API keys to each team, each team gets a LiteLLM virtual key scoped to their allowed models and budget. The Proxy translates virtual key requests to the appropriate provider key at the gateway level. The operational benefit is centralized rotation: when a provider key needs to be rotated, only the Proxy config changes — all downstream consumers using virtual keys are unaffected. The failure mode is using virtual keys without configuring a master_key on the proxy, which leaves the /key/generate endpoint open without authentication.

LiteLLM's moderation endpoint integration (litellm.moderation()) routes content through OpenAI's moderation API or configurable alternatives before the actual completion call. When integrated into a production pipeline, the moderation call adds a full round-trip latency hit (100-300ms) to every request. The architectural tradeoff: synchronous pre-call moderation is the safest approach for user-generated inputs but makes latency SLOs harder to meet. Alternatives include: (1) async fire-and-forget moderation that flags content post-completion for review rather than blocking; (2) running moderation only on the first message in a session (not every turn); (3) using a smaller, faster local moderation model for pre-screening with full moderation on flagged content only. The choice depends on whether false negatives (letting harmful content through) or latency is the higher-cost failure mode.

LiteLLM's exception mapping translates provider-specific errors into OpenAI-compatible exceptions (AuthenticationError, RateLimitError, ContextWindowExceededError, etc.). However, the mapping is incomplete for newer providers and model-specific errors: Anthropic's 'overloaded_error' maps to a generic ServiceUnavailableError rather than a distinct error class, and Google Vertex's quota errors sometimes map to InternalServerError instead of RateLimitError. Code that catches RateLimitError for retry logic will silently miss Vertex quota errors if they map to InternalServerError. The defensive pattern is to catch the LiteLLM base exception class and inspect the error message string for rate-limit keywords, not rely solely on exception type matching. Logging the raw status_code and provider_error_code from every exception is essential for debugging mapping gaps.

LiteLLM provides a unified Python SDK that maps 100+ LLM provider APIs to a single OpenAI-compatible interface, solving the vendor lock-in and API fragmentation problem in multi-provider AI applications. Without LiteLLM, switching from OpenAI to Anthropic requires changing API client initialization, parameter names (max_tokens vs max_tokens_to_sample), response field paths, streaming event types, and error classes. LiteLLM normalizes all of these: completion(), acompletion(), and embedding() work identically across OpenAI, Anthropic, Cohere, Google, AWS Bedrock, Azure, and 90+ other providers. The normalization layer adds ~5ms overhead per call, negligible versus LLM latency. Primary use case: experimentation across providers and production fallback routing without code changes.

statement: LiteLLM's automatic retry logic retries on network errors but does not retry on provider-specific quota errors (HTTP 429) by default — you must configure num_retries and enable retry_on_rate_limit=True. Without this configuration, a 429 from OpenAI causes LiteLLM to surface the error immediately to the caller instead of waiting and retrying. Additionally, LiteLLM's default retry uses fixed intervals; configure with litellm.retry_policy={'retries': 3, 'delay': 2, 'backoff': 2} to use exponential backoff. A common production failure pattern: load testing reveals 100% success rate at 10 req/s but 15% failure rate at 20 req/s because rate limit retries were not configured, and the quota buffer is exhausted faster than manual retry logic catches up.

statement: Setting up LiteLLM Router for production load balancing across multiple API keys and providers: (1) pip install litellm. (2) Configure router: from litellm import Router; router = Router(model_list=[{'model_name':'gpt-4','litellm_params':{'model':'gpt-4','api_key':KEY1}},{'model_name':'gpt-4','litellm_params':{'model':'gpt-4','api_key':KEY2}},{'model_name':'gpt-4','litellm_params':{'model':'claude-3-opus-20240229','api_key':ANTHROPIC_KEY}}]). (3) Call: response = await router.acompletion(model='gpt-4', messages=[...]). (4) Router applies cooldown on failed keys (default 60s), round-robin across healthy keys. (5) Monitor with router.get_available_deployments(). This pattern distributes load across multiple API keys of the same provider (bypassing per-key rate limits) and provides automatic failover to backup providers.

statement: LiteLLM's tool/function calling support is inconsistent across providers due to fundamental API differences. Anthropic's tool use API requires a separate tool_result message type in the conversation history that OpenAI's function calling does not use — LiteLLM attempts to translate between formats, but nested tool calls, parallel tool calls, and tool call error handling diverge between providers. OpenAI allows multiple tool calls in a single response; Anthropic Claude 3 allows it but Claude 2 does not. When building tool-calling agents with LiteLLM, always test with each target provider separately — the translation layer may silently drop tool results or reorder them. For complex multi-tool agents, LangChain or LlamaIndex's abstraction layers above LiteLLM are more reliable than using LiteLLM's raw tool call translation directly.

statement: LiteLLM fallback routing pattern for high availability: configure a primary provider (GPT-4 Turbo) with fallbacks to secondary (Claude 3 Sonnet) and tertiary (Gemini 1.5 Pro) providers. litellm.completion(model='gpt-4-turbo', messages=[...], fallbacks=['claude-3-sonnet-20240229', 'gemini/gemini-1.5-pro']). When GPT-4 Turbo returns an error (network error, 5xx, 429 after retries), LiteLLM automatically retries with claude-3-sonnet, then gemini if that also fails. This achieves 99.9%+ availability by spreading failure modes across independent providers. Key monitoring: litellm.success_callback and litellm.failure_callback hooks report which provider served each request, enabling SLA tracking. Production deployments should log provider per request to detect when fallbacks activate unusually frequently.

statement: LiteLLM's max_tokens parameter normalization fails for AWS Bedrock models (claude-3-*) when the value exceeds the model's actual output token limit. LiteLLM passes max_tokens directly to Bedrock's max_tokens field, but Bedrock enforces a 4096 token output limit for Claude 3 Haiku/Sonnet even if the model technically supports 8192 tokens. Bedrock returns a ValidationException (not a standard 400 error) that LiteLLM's error mapping translates to a generic LiteLLM exception rather than a max_tokens_exceeded exception. Debugging this requires inspecting the raw error message. Fix: always set max_tokens ≤ 4096 for Claude models on Bedrock, regardless of what the model card advertises. The limit is a Bedrock platform constraint, not a model capability constraint.

statement: LiteLLM Proxy (litellm --model gpt-3.5-turbo --port 4000) starts a local OpenAI-compatible server that proxies to any configured LLM provider, enabling tools that only support OpenAI's API (LangChain, AutoGPT, Open Interpreter, etc.) to use alternative providers without code changes. The proxy handles API key management, request logging, rate limiting, and provider routing transparently. Enterprise features in LiteLLM Proxy: team-based budget limits, per-user token quotas, audit logging, virtual keys (rotate underlying provider keys without updating client applications). Start with Docker: docker run ghcr.io/berriai/litellm:main-latest --model anthropic/claude-3-sonnet-20240229 to run Claude behind an OpenAI-compatible API endpoint.

statement: LiteLLM's streaming response normalization breaks for Anthropic Claude 3 when using tool use with streaming enabled. Anthropic's streaming format for tool calls uses input_json_delta events that accumulate a JSON string incrementally, while OpenAI uses function.arguments delta chunks. LiteLLM normalizes these to OpenAI's delta format, but partial JSON strings in intermediate chunks are not valid JSON. If client code tries to parse each streaming chunk's tool call arguments as JSON (a common mistake), it will fail on all chunks except the last. Correct streaming tool call handling: accumulate the arguments string across all chunks, then parse the complete string after the [DONE] event. This is the same requirement for OpenAI streaming tool calls but the error is more common with LiteLLM because developers expect the translation to handle it transparently.

statement: Configuring LiteLLM with cost tracking and budget alerts: (1) Enable spend tracking: litellm.success_callback=['langfuse'] or custom callback. (2) Set budget limits: litellm.max_budget=100 (dollars) or per-model: litellm.model_cost={'gpt-4':{'input_cost_per_token':0.00001}}. (3) Monitor via LiteLLM UI (bundled with litellm proxy --ui). (4) Set alerts: LITELLM_EMAIL_FOR_COST_ALERTS env var triggers email when budget exceeds threshold. (5) Per-user tracking: pass user= parameter in completion() calls; LiteLLM tracks spend per user_id. (6) Export to PostgreSQL: litellm --config config.yaml with database_url for persistent tracking. This configuration provides real-time cost visibility, preventing runaway API spending common in development environments where requests-per-day are not monitored.

statement: Multi-provider semantic search pipeline with LiteLLM: (1) Use LiteLLM embedding() to generate embeddings through a unified interface, supporting OpenAI text-embedding-3-large, Cohere embed-english-v3.0, and Vertex AI text-embedding-004 with the same code. (2) Router selects the cheapest available embedding provider with sufficient quality for the task. (3) Embeddings stored in Qdrant with provider metadata. (4) At query time, use the same embedding provider as was used for document indexing (provider mismatch causes dimension mismatches or similarity score degradation). (5) LiteLLM's caching layer (litellm.cache = Cache(type='redis')) caches embedding results for repeated queries, reducing costs 40-70% in typical search workloads. Key constraint: different providers produce embeddings of different dimensions — establish a single provider for each index and never mix.

statement: LiteLLM's response caching with Redis produces cache misses for requests that are semantically identical but have trivial formatting differences (extra whitespace, different message role capitalization, reordered system prompt paragraphs). The cache key is computed from the exact JSON representation of the request, so any byte-level difference produces a different cache key. This defeats caching for applications where prompts are dynamically assembled with slight variations. Fix: normalize prompts before caching (strip trailing whitespace, normalize message ordering, sort metadata fields) in a pre-processing layer before passing to LiteLLM. Alternatively, implement semantic caching by storing prompt embeddings and retrieving cache hits based on cosine similarity >0.99. LiteLLM's built-in semantic cache uses this approach with the similarity_threshold parameter.

statement: LiteLLM's completion() function signature mirrors OpenAI's with additional parameters: litellm.completion(model='gpt-4-turbo', messages=[{'role':'system','content':'You are helpful'},{'role':'user','content':'Hello'}], temperature=0.7, max_tokens=1000, stream=False, timeout=30, api_base=None, api_key=None, num_retries=3, fallbacks=['claude-3-sonnet-20240229'], metadata={'user':'john','trace_id':'abc123'}). The metadata field passes through to observability callbacks (Langfuse, OpenTelemetry) for request tracking. Provider-specific parameters can be passed via extra_body={}: litellm.completion(..., extra_body={'anthropic_version':'bedrock-2023-05-31'}) for Bedrock-specific parameters. Async version: await litellm.acompletion(...) for async contexts. Return type: ModelResponse object with .choices[0].message.content, .usage.total_tokens, .model.

statement: LiteLLM async rate limiting with asyncio fails silently when the semaphore count is misconfigured for the provider's actual limits. A common pattern uses asyncio.Semaphore(50) to limit concurrent requests to 50, but OpenAI's TPM (tokens per minute) limit is the actual binding constraint, not the request count limit. With async batching, 50 concurrent 4K-token requests = 200K tokens in-flight simultaneously, easily exceeding OpenAI's 40K TPM limit for tier-1 accounts. The requests queue in OpenAI's API (not locally), leading to 429 errors that retry and accumulate. Fix: implement token-bucket rate limiting based on estimated token consumption, not just request count. Calculate estimated_tokens = len(prompt) / 4 (rough token estimate) and enforce TPM limit with a rolling window token bucket.

statement: Observability integration with LiteLLM and Langfuse for LLM operation monitoring: set litellm.success_callback=['langfuse'] and litellm.failure_callback=['langfuse'], configure LANGFUSE_PUBLIC_KEY and LANGFUSE_SECRET_KEY environment variables. Every LiteLLM call automatically logs: model name, provider, prompt tokens, completion tokens, cost, latency, and full request/response to Langfuse. For multi-turn conversations, pass trace_id in metadata to group related calls into a single trace. Production dashboard shows: cost per model per day, P50/P99 latency by provider, error rate by provider, and token efficiency (response tokens / prompt tokens). This observability setup requires zero code changes to existing LiteLLM calls beyond the one-time callback configuration.

statement: LiteLLM supports streaming with a unified delta format across all providers, normalizing Anthropic's text_delta, Cohere's text events, and Google's candidates[0].content.parts[0].text to OpenAI's choices[0].delta.content. Client code using: for chunk in litellm.completion(..., stream=True): print(chunk.choices[0].delta.content or '', end='') works with every supported provider. The normalization handles edge cases: null delta.content at stream start (many providers send a role: 'assistant' delta first), empty string at end of generation, and the final [DONE] token. Streaming support was added incrementally — always test streaming for new provider+model combinations before deploying to production, as new providers sometimes have streaming bugs in early releases.

statement: LiteLLM's model name mapping is case-sensitive and requires exact provider prefix syntax. 'anthropic/claude-3-sonnet-20240229' works; 'Claude-3-Sonnet' or 'claude-sonnet' without the anthropic/ prefix fails with a 'Provider Not Found' error. For Azure models, the format is 'azure/deployment-name' where deployment-name is the Azure deployment name (not the underlying model name). For Bedrock: 'bedrock/anthropic.claude-3-sonnet-20240229-v1:0' (note the colon and version suffix). For Vertex AI: 'vertex_ai/gemini-1.5-pro' (note underscore in provider name). These naming inconsistencies arise from vendor API differences. Maintain a mapping table in application configuration: logical_name → litellm_model_string for all models in use, and test each mapping at deployment startup with a minimal probe request.

statement: Using LiteLLM with Azure OpenAI Service: (1) Set environment variables: AZURE_API_KEY, AZURE_API_BASE (e.g., https://myresource.openai.azure.com), AZURE_API_VERSION (e.g., 2024-02-15-preview). (2) Call: litellm.completion(model='azure/my-gpt4-deployment', messages=[...]). The model parameter format is 'azure/{deployment_name}'. (3) For multiple Azure deployments: configure via Router with api_base per deployment to point to different Azure resources. (4) Content filtering: Azure applies additional safety filters; configure litellm.drop_params=True to strip unsupported parameters that cause Azure to reject requests. (5) Managed Identity auth: set api_key='managed_identity' and ensure the service has Cognitive Services OpenAI User role assigned in Azure IAM. (6) Verify connectivity: litellm.completion(model='azure/deployment', messages=[{'role':'user','content':'hi'}], max_tokens=5).

statement: LiteLLM's completion() does not support all OpenAI parameters for all providers — unsupported parameters are silently dropped (with litellm.drop_params=True) or cause a 400 error (default behavior). Parameters that are OpenAI-specific and not available across providers: presence_penalty, frequency_penalty (not supported by Anthropic), logit_bias (not supported by most providers), n (number of completions, only OpenAI), best_of (OpenAI only), logprobs (OpenAI only, partially supported by some). Before using advanced sampling parameters, check litellm.get_supported_openai_params(model='your-model') which returns a list of supported parameters for that model. For provider-agnostic code, stick to: model, messages, temperature, max_tokens, stream, stop — these are universally supported.

statement: A/B testing framework for LLM models using LiteLLM Router: configure router with multiple models and custom routing strategy: router = Router(model_list=[{'model_name':'experiment','litellm_params':{'model':'gpt-4-turbo'},'model_info':{'traffic_split':70}},{'model_name':'experiment','litellm_params':{'model':'claude-3-sonnet-20240229'},'model_info':{'traffic_split':30}}], routing_strategy='weighted_round_robin'). 70% of 'experiment' model requests go to GPT-4 Turbo, 30% to Claude 3 Sonnet. Log which model served each request via Langfuse with metadata={'model_variant': response.model}. Analyze quality (human ratings or automated eval), cost, and latency per variant. After 1000 requests per variant, statistical power is sufficient for 5% effect size detection. This pattern enables evidence-based model selection without blue-green full deployment switches.

statement: LiteLLM's Proxy server's virtual keys feature (team-based API keys that map to underlying provider keys) has a race condition in concurrent request processing when the rate limit budget is shared across multiple proxy instances. If two proxy instances simultaneously check a team's remaining budget and both see it as positive, both allow the request, temporarily exceeding the configured team budget. This bug manifests in high-concurrency scenarios with shared Redis state — the Redis INCR operation is atomic but the read-check-update pattern in the budget enforcement is not. Fix: use Redis INCR with EXPIRE for atomic budget decrements (decrement first, check if below negative threshold, rollback if exceeded). Alternatively, enforce rate limits at the single-instance proxy level by using sticky routing (route all requests for a team to the same proxy instance).

statement: LiteLLM text moderation pipeline: use litellm.completion() with a dedicated moderation model (openai/text-moderation-latest or a custom endpoint) before sending user input to the main LLM. Implementation: def safe_complete(messages): moderation_result = litellm.moderation(input=messages[-1]['content']); if moderation_result.results[0].flagged: raise ValueError('Content policy violation'); return litellm.completion(model='gpt-4', messages=messages). For production: add moderation to both user input (before LLM call) and LLM output (after generation, catches jailbreak outputs). Use async: run moderation and LLM completion concurrently for user inputs that are unlikely to be flagged, reducing latency by the moderation call overhead (typically 100-200ms). Moderation callback via litellm.success_callback allows post-hoc logging of all completions for compliance audit.

statement: LiteLLM supports Google Vertex AI models (Gemini 1.5 Pro, Gemini 1.5 Flash, Claude 3 on Vertex) through a Google Cloud authentication adapter that uses Application Default Credentials (ADC) or service account JSON. Configuration: GOOGLE_APPLICATION_CREDENTIALS=/path/to/service-account.json or gcloud auth application-default login. Model prefix: 'vertex_ai/gemini-1.5-pro'. Vertex AI provides enterprise features unavailable in Google AI Studio: VPC Service Controls, CMEK encryption, audit logging, and on-premises data processing. LiteLLM abstracts the Vertex AI SDK's verbose authentication boilerplate into a 2-line configuration. For production: use Workload Identity Federation (not service account JSON files) to avoid credential rotation issues in GKE deployments.

statement: LiteLLM's response object normalization drops provider-specific fields that are not part of the OpenAI schema. Anthropic returns a stop_reason field with values 'end_turn', 'max_tokens', 'stop_sequence', 'tool_use'; LiteLLM normalizes this to OpenAI's finish_reason values 'stop', 'length', 'tool_calls'. However, Anthropic's 'end_turn' has subtly different semantics than OpenAI's 'stop' — 'end_turn' means the model chose to stop, while 'stop' includes matching a stop sequence. Applications that distinguish these cases for routing decisions (e.g., 'retry if stopped by stop sequence but not if end_turn') need to access the raw provider response. Access it via: response._hidden_params['original_response'] to get the provider-native response object before normalization.

statement: Configuring LiteLLM Proxy as a Kubernetes sidecar for LLM request management: (1) Deploy litellm proxy as a sidecar container in application pods: image: ghcr.io/berriai/litellm. (2) Configure via config.yaml volume mount: model_list, router_settings (cooldown_time: 60, allowed_fails: 3), general_settings (master_key for auth). (3) Application connects to localhost:4000 instead of LLM provider APIs directly. (4) Set resources: requests cpu:100m memory:256Mi, limits cpu:500m memory:512Mi. (5) Add liveness probe: httpGet /health/liveliness (6) Sidecar pattern enables per-application LLM configuration, audit logging, and rate limiting without infrastructure changes. The sidecar approach is simpler than a centralized gateway for teams that want independent LLM configuration per microservice, with the trade-off of N proxy instances instead of one shared gateway.

statement: LiteLLM's semantic caching uses embedding similarity to find cache hits, but this requires an embedding model call for every request — adding 50-200ms latency to compute the query embedding before checking the cache. For high-frequency, low-latency applications, semantic caching can be slower than no caching if the cache hit rate is low. The break-even point: cache_latency_overhead < average_cache_miss_latency × cache_hit_rate. For typical LLM calls with 2000ms average latency and 200ms cache overhead, semantic caching is beneficial when hit rate > 10%. For sub-100ms tasks (short completions, moderation calls), the overhead makes semantic caching counterproductive unless hit rate exceeds 50%. Monitor cache metrics: litellm.cache.get_cache_stats() reports hit rate for informed tuning decisions.

statement: Cost-optimized model routing with LiteLLM: implement a tiered routing strategy that routes requests to the cheapest model that can handle the task. Classification logic: if estimated_complexity == 'simple': model='gpt-3.5-turbo'; elif task_type == 'code': model='deepseek/deepseek-coder'; elif requires_vision: model='gpt-4o-mini'; else: model='claude-3-haiku-20240307'. Use LiteLLM Router's routing_strategy='cost-based-routing' which automatically routes to the cheapest model from the model_list that meets quality criteria. Combine with LiteLLM's litellm.model_alias_map to create logical model names (cheap, medium, powerful) that map to provider-specific models, enabling provider switching without application code changes.

statement: LiteLLM fails to correctly handle Cohere's multi-step tool use where the model returns a SEARCH_QUERY generation type before the final TEXT response. LiteLLM's response normalization expects either a direct content response or tool_calls, but Cohere's Command R+ model uses intermediate SEARCH_QUERY events in agentic mode that don't map cleanly to OpenAI's tool call schema. The result: LiteLLM returns a partial response with empty content when Cohere is in agentic search mode. Fix: use Cohere's native Python SDK (cohere.Client) for Cohere Command R+ agentic tasks rather than routing through LiteLLM, and use LiteLLM only for Cohere's standard generation tasks. statement: LiteLLM's batch completion supports processing multiple independent requests in parallel using asyncio: import asyncio; results = await asyncio.gather(*[litellm.acompletion(model='gpt-4', messages=msgs) for msgs in batch_of_messages]). This parallelizes N independent requests, achieving N× throughput improvement versus sequential calling. For large batches (>100 requests), use asyncio.Semaphore to limit concurrency and avoid overwhelming the provider's rate limits. LiteLLM also supports provider-native batch APIs: for OpenAI's batch API, use litellm.batch_completion() which submits to OpenAI's /v1/batches endpoint for 50% cost reduction with 24-hour processing time. The batch API path is distinct from gather-based parallelism — choose based on whether real-time results are needed.

statement: Enterprise LLM gateway with LiteLLM Proxy for centralized management: (1) Single LiteLLM Proxy instance behind an ALB serves all internal services. (2) Virtual keys issued per team with budget limits and model access controls: team-a gets claude-3-opus (expensive), team-b gets claude-3-haiku (cheap). (3) All requests logged to PostgreSQL with team_id, user_id, model, tokens, cost, latency. (4) Prometheus metrics endpoint enables real-time cost dashboards in Grafana. (5) Guardrails configuration blocks PII in requests/responses using presidio integration. (6) Model aliases map abstract names (fast-chat → haiku, smart-chat → sonnet) to concrete models, allowing model upgrades without client changes. statement: LiteLLM's token counting with litellm.token_counter() underestimates token counts for Anthropic Claude models when messages contain image content blocks. The token counter uses tiktoken (OpenAI's tokenizer) as a proxy for all models, which cannot accurately count tokens for image inputs because image token counts depend on the image dimensions and the model's vision encoding (Anthropic charges ~1000-2000 tokens per image regardless of content). Underestimated token counts lead to context overflow errors when the assembled prompt is sent to the API, or unexpected cost overruns if budget is managed by estimated tokens. For image-containing requests to Claude, use Anthropic's documented image token formulas directly: width × height / 750 tiles × tokens_per_tile.

statement: LiteLLM Proxy stores virtual key metadata and spend tracking in a PostgreSQL database rather than in-memory to survive restarts and support multi-instance deployments. PostgreSQL provides durability, concurrent read-write safety for multi-instance deployments (multiple proxy instances behind a load balancer sharing the same database), and queryability for cost analysis queries. statement: Implementing custom LiteLLM callbacks for request/response logging and alerting: (1) Create callback class: class MyCallback(CustomLogger): def log_success_event(self, kwargs, response_obj, start_time, end_time): # log to your system. (2) Register: litellm.callbacks=[MyCallback()]. (3) kwargs contains: model, messages, api_key (hashed), call_type, metadata. (4) response_obj contains: ModelResponse with usage, choices. (5) For async: inherit from AsyncCustomLogger and implement async_log_success_event. (6) For alerting on high cost: check response_obj.usage.total_tokens × model_cost_per_token in the callback and fire a webhook if it exceeds a per-request threshold. statement: LiteLLM's AWS Bedrock integration fails with 'NoCredentialsError' when running in Lambda or ECS without explicitly configuring the boto3 credential chain order. LiteLLM uses boto3 for Bedrock calls, which follows AWS credential chain: environment variables → ~/.aws/credentials → Instance Metadata Service (IMDS). In Lambda with IAM role, credentials are available via IMDS but boto3 may timeout on IMDS if the Lambda was created with restrictive IMDSv2 settings. Fix: set AWS_METADATA_SERVICE_NUM_ATTEMPTS=3 and AWS_METADATA_SERVICE_TIMEOUT=10 environment variables to increase IMDS retry tolerance, or explicitly pass: litellm.completion(model='bedrock/...', aws_access_key_id=..., aws_secret_access_key=..., aws_region_name='us-east-1') when credential chain detection fails.

statement: LiteLLM with OpenTelemetry for distributed tracing in microservice architectures: configure litellm.callbacks=['otel'] with OTEL_EXPORTER_OTLP_ENDPOINT and OTEL_SERVICE_NAME environment variables. Each LiteLLM call creates a span with attributes: llm.model, llm.provider, llm.prompt_tokens, llm.completion_tokens, llm.cost. Pass the parent trace context via metadata={'trace_context': opentelemetry.trace.get_current_span().get_span_context()} to correctly link LLM calls to the originating service trace. statement: LiteLLM Router's 'least-busy' routing strategy does not account for the different throughput capacities of different model deployments. Better approach: use routing_strategy='latency-based-routing' combined with deployment-level capacity hints, or implement custom routing logic using LiteLLM's RouterStrategy base class. statement: LiteLLM's load balancing with the Router fails to detect 'silent failures' where a provider returns HTTP 200 but with an error in the response body. statement:

statement: LiteLLM's Proxy fails to start on Python 3.12+ with ImportError for some dependencies when installed via pip install 'litellm[proxy]'. The proxy extras include several packages with C extensions that may not have published wheels for Python 3.12 at the time of a LiteLLM release. Fix: use Docker image instead of pip install for proxy (the Docker image pins working dependency versions), or explicitly pin Python to 3.11 in the deployment environment. statement: LiteLLM with Guardrails for PII protection: configure a pre-call hook using the PresidioGuardrail from litellm-guardrails: guardrail = PresidioGuardrail(guardrail_name='pii-guard', guardrail_mode='pre_call'). This hook analyzes request messages before sending to the LLM, replacing detected PII (names, emails, phone numbers, SSNs) with placeholder tokens ([NAME], [EMAIL]). Post-call guardrail reverses the substitution in the response. For compliance requirements (HIPAA, GDPR), this ensures no PII is transmitted to external LLM APIs while maintaining conversational context. Limitations: Presidio's NER models have 85-95% PII detection accuracy depending on PII type — some PII may slip through, especially non-standard formats. statement: LiteLLM's caching with Redis requires exact prompt match by default, which fails for LLM applications that include dynamic elements like current timestamps, session IDs, or incrementing request numbers in messages. These dynamic elements change every request, preventing cache hits even when the semantically meaningful content is identical. Applications must remove non-semantic fields before using LiteLLM caching. Configure cache key extraction: litellm.cache = Cache(type='redis', supported_call_types=['acompletion'], exclude_headers=['timestamp', 'session_id']) where the exclude_headers list removes these fields from cache key computation. Alternatively, structure prompts to isolate static (cacheable) system prompts from dynamic user context, using litellm's cache_control parameter to mark which message roles should be excluded from cache key computation.

statement: LiteLLM supports image input (vision) for all multimodal providers through a unified image format: messages=[{'role':'user','content':[{'type':'text','text':'What is in this image?'},{'type':'image_url','image_url':{'url':'data:image/jpeg;base64,BASE64_DATA'}}]}]. This format works with GPT-4o, Claude 3 (Haiku/Sonnet/Opus), Gemini 1.5 Pro/Flash, and Llava models. Provider-specific image handling (Anthropic requires source.media_type + source.data, OpenAI requires url) is abstracted by LiteLLM. URL-based images (non-base64) work with providers that accept public URLs; for private images, base64 encoding is universal. Image cost varies significantly: GPT-4o charges per tile (512×512 grid); Claude charges per 'image token' based on dimensions; Gemini charges based on input tokens. LiteLLM's cost tracking accounts for image tokens if the model cost definition includes vision pricing.

statement: LiteLLM's Router with cooldown_time set too high (>300s) creates availability gaps during provider incidents. When a provider has a 5-minute incident (all requests return 503), the router marks all deployments for that provider as cooled down and routes to fallbacks. When the provider recovers, the router keeps routing to fallbacks for the remaining cooldown period, missing cheaper or higher-quality capacity. Optimal cooldown_time: 60-120 seconds, which is long enough to avoid hammering a degraded provider but short enough to recover quickly. Also set allowed_fails_policy to 'exponential_cooldown' rather than fixed: after 3 failures, cooldown starts at 30s, doubles on each failure, resets on success. This adaptive approach handles both brief spikes and sustained incidents.

statement: Migrating from direct OpenAI SDK to LiteLLM with minimal code changes: (1) Replace: from openai import OpenAI; client = OpenAI() with import litellm. (2) Replace: client.chat.completions.create(model='gpt-4', messages=[...]) with litellm.completion(model='gpt-4', messages=[...]). (3) The response object is a drop-in replacement — response.choices[0].message.content works identically. (4) Error classes: replace openai.RateLimitError with litellm.RateLimitError (same attributes). (5) For async: replace AsyncOpenAI with litellm.acompletion (no client instantiation needed). (6) Add fallbacks and routing gradually: start with litellm.completion identical to OpenAI, then add fallbacks=['claude-3-haiku'] as needed. This migration is typically achievable in <30 minutes for a codebase with centralized LLM call logic.

statement: Real-time content personalization with LiteLLM multi-provider routing: (1) User profile (age, interests, language preference, reading level) encoded in the system prompt template. (2) LiteLLM Router selects model based on content type: structured data extraction → GPT-4 (highest accuracy), casual chat → Claude 3 Haiku (low latency), technical content → Deepseek (domain strength), non-English → multilingual model. (3) Each request tagged with user_id and content_type for cost attribution. (4) A/B testing layer (5% of requests) routes to experimental models to measure personalization effectiveness. statement: LiteLLM breaks when providers update their model version identifiers without maintaining backward compatibility in the model name. For example, OpenAI periodically retires old model aliases (gpt-4-0613 → gpt-4-turbo) and Anthropic retires dated versions (claude-3-sonnet-20240229 gets deprecated). When a model ID is retired, LiteLLM's API call returns a 404 (model not found), not a 410 (gone). Applications that hardcode specific model version strings (e.g., 'claude-3-sonnet-20240229' instead of 'claude-3-sonnet-latest') will break silently after model retirement without code changes. Best practice: use provider-maintained 'latest' aliases for non-production environments (gpt-4-latest, claude-3-sonnet-latest) and freeze to specific dated versions only after validating production behavior, with a quarterly audit process to check for model deprecation notices.

statement: LiteLLM's get_max_tokens() utility returns the maximum context window for any supported model: litellm.get_max_tokens('gpt-4-turbo') returns 128000. This enables context-aware prompt assembly: if total_tokens(system_prompt + context + history) > litellm.get_max_tokens(model) × 0.9, truncate context before sending. LiteLLM's token_counter() combined with get_max_tokens() enables precise context management: calculate remaining_tokens = max_tokens - token_counter(model, messages); use remaining_tokens to size the user's current message or retrieved context. This is more reliable than hardcoded context limits because get_max_tokens() is updated when models expand their context windows, avoiding the need to manually update limits as providers release new context tiers.

statement: LiteLLM with Prometheus and Grafana for production SLO monitoring: configure litellm.callbacks=['prometheus'] with PROMETHEUS_URL. Exported metrics: litellm_requests_total{model, provider, status}, litellm_request_duration_seconds{model, provider}, litellm_tokens_total{model, type}, litellm_spend_total{model, provider, team}. Define SLOs: P95 latency <3s for interactive requests, error rate <1% per model per hour. Grafana alert rules: avg_over_time(litellm_request_duration_seconds[5m]) > 3 → page on-call; rate(litellm_requests_total{status='error'}[5m]) / rate(litellm_requests_total[5m]) > 0.01 → alert. Dashboard panels: cost per hour trend (budget burn rate), per-provider error rate heatmap, latency percentiles. This monitoring setup enables SLA management for LLM-powered applications similar to standard microservice observability.

statement: LiteLLM Proxy's database connection pool exhaustion causes request failures during load spikes when using PostgreSQL with default settings. LiteLLM Proxy opens one connection per worker process (default workers = CPU_count × 2 via uvicorn); with 16 worker processes and a PostgreSQL max_connections=100, only ~6 proxy instances can run before hitting the connection limit. Under load, connection acquisition times out, causing 503 errors from the proxy even when the LLM providers are healthy. Fix: configure LITELLM_DATABASE_POOL_SIZE=5 (connections per worker), use PgBouncer connection pooler between proxy and PostgreSQL, or increase PostgreSQL max_connections with corresponding shared_buffers tuning. Monitor active connections with SELECT count(*) FROM pg_stat_activity WHERE state='active'; alarms at >80% of max_connections.

statement: LiteLLM uses a provider-specific transformation layer (not a simple configuration table) because different LLM APIs have fundamentally different request/response schemas that cannot be reconciled by field renaming alone. Anthropic requires a separate user_id parameter for child content filtering; AWS Bedrock wraps the request body in an anthropic_version field; Azure adds deployment_id and api_version query parameters; Google Vertex AI uses OAuth2 instead of API keys and a different service endpoint per model. These differences require provider-specific code paths for authentication, request serialization, and response deserialization. LiteLLM's transformation layer abstracts these differences so that application code sees one consistent interface, while provider-specific handling lives in provider adapter modules (litellm/main.py provider-specific functions).

statement: LiteLLM's Router does not support stateful routing where subsequent requests in a conversation must go to the same model deployment as earlier turns. With round-robin or least-busy routing, turn 1 might go to GPT-4 key A, turn 2 to GPT-4 key B. For providers where context is stored server-side (OpenAI Assistants, some fine-tuned model deployments), this breaks the conversation. Solution: implement sticky sessions at the application layer by hashing the conversation_id to a consistent deployment index: deployment_index = hash(conversation_id) % len(router.deployments). This ensures all turns of a conversation go to the same deployment. LiteLLM Router does not provide built-in conversation affinity — it must be implemented in the application routing logic.

statement: LiteLLM integration with LangSmith for debugging complex LLM chains: (1) Set LANGCHAIN_API_KEY and LANGCHAIN_TRACING_V2=true environment variables. (2) Configure: litellm.callbacks=['langsmith']. (3) Each LiteLLM call creates a LangSmith run with full request/response content, token counts, and latency. (4) For multi-step chains, pass run_id in metadata to group related calls: litellm.completion(..., metadata={'parent_run_id': parent_run.id}). (5) Use LangSmith's comparison view to debug quality differences between model versions. (6) LangSmith's dataset feature: curate failing examples from production traces into evaluation datasets for regression testing. This integration is particularly useful when debugging why a specific user's interaction failed — production traces are searchable by user_id (passed in metadata) with full prompt/response visibility.

statement: LiteLLM's error handling swallows provider-specific error details when mapping to standard LiteLLM exception types. Anthropic returns detailed error messages in the response body like 'input length 250000 exceeds maximum context length 200000' — LiteLLM maps this to litellm.ContextWindowExceededError but the specific token counts are dropped from the exception message. This makes it impossible to calculate how much context to trim when handling ContextWindowExceededError programmatically. Workaround: catch the exception, inspect exception.llm_provider and exception.response to access the raw provider error message, then parse the token counts from the provider-specific error string. The information is present in the exception's response attribute but requires provider-specific parsing logic, defeating the abstraction.

statement: Provider cost comparison framework with LiteLLM: create a benchmarking script that sends identical prompts to N providers and records response quality, latency, and cost. Use litellm.completion() with each provider model string; record usage.prompt_tokens, usage.completion_tokens, and calculate cost = litellm.completion_cost(completion_response=response). For quality measurement: use an LLM judge model to rate each response, or use automated metrics (ROUGE for summarization, exact match for factual Q&A). Build a cost-quality Pareto frontier across providers. Update monthly as providers change pricing. This data-driven approach avoids both cost over-spending (using GPT-4 for tasks Claude 3 Haiku handles equally well) and false economy (using cheap models for tasks that require high capability, increasing error rate costs downstream).

statement: LiteLLM supports structured output (JSON mode) through two mechanisms: (1) For OpenAI and compatible providers: pass response_format={'type':'json_object'} to enforce JSON output. (2) For other providers: use the instructor library on top of LiteLLM for Pydantic model validation. import litellm; import instructor; client = instructor.from_litellm(litellm.completion); response = client(model='claude-3-sonnet-20240229', response_model=MyPydanticModel, messages=[...]). The instructor integration patches LiteLLM's completion to automatically retry with error feedback until the response parses successfully as the specified Pydantic model. statement: LiteLLM's async client (litellm.acompletion) creates a new httpx.AsyncClient for each call rather than reusing a connection pool, causing TCP connection overhead that becomes significant at high request rates. At 100 requests/second, creating 100 new TCP connections/second to the same OpenAI endpoint adds 10-50ms per request for SSL handshake. Fix: configure a shared httpx.AsyncClient with a connection pool: litellm.aclient = httpx.AsyncClient(limits=httpx.Limits(max_connections=100, keepalive_expiry=30)). Alternatively, use the OpenAI provider directly with openai.AsyncOpenAI(max_retries=0) for high-throughput OpenAI-specific workloads where LiteLLM's overhead is not acceptable. This is a known LiteLLM performance limitation for high-QPS scenarios.

statement: Setting up LiteLLM with AWS SageMaker endpoints for fine-tuned model inference: (1) Deploy model to SageMaker endpoint and note the endpoint name. statement: LiteLLM's Router does not guarantee FIFO (first-in, first-out) request ordering when multiple requests are queued for the same model deployment. Under load, requests from a semaphore-limited queue may be reordered based on Python asyncio's internal scheduling. For applications where response order matters (streaming responses to numbered questions, batch processing with sequential IDs), use litellm.batch_completion_models_all_responses() which maintains response ordering by gathering results and sorting by request index. Alternatively, tag each request with a monotonic sequence number in metadata and re-sort responses by sequence number in the application layer. FIFO ordering is only guaranteed within a single asyncio.gather() call, not across Router-mediated concurrent requests.

statement: Multi-language customer support system with LiteLLM: (1) User message language detected with langdetect library (<5ms). (2) LiteLLM Router routes to optimal model per language: English → Claude 3 Sonnet (best English instruction following), Chinese → Qwen-2.5-72B via DashScope (best Chinese reasoning), Japanese → GPT-4o (balanced multilingual), Spanish/French/German → Llama-3.1-70B via local deployment (cost optimization for well-supported Romance languages). (3) System prompt translated to the detected language to maximize instruction following. (4) Response quality monitored per language by sampling and rating with an LLM judge in the same language. statement: LiteLLM's Proxy health check endpoint /health returns 200 even when the underlying database connection is down, as long as the HTTP server is running. This means load balancer health checks based on /health will keep routing traffic to a proxy instance that cannot write audit logs or enforce team budgets (because the database is unreachable). Use /health/readiness for readiness probes in Kubernetes — this endpoint checks database connectivity and returns 503 if the database is down. The distinction between /health (liveness) and /health/readiness (readiness) follows Kubernetes probe semantics: liveness probe failures restart the pod, readiness probe failures remove it from load balancer rotation without restarting.

statement: Cost-aware dynamic context assembly with LiteLLM: before assembling the final prompt, calculate the marginal cost of additional context tokens. For gpt-4-turbo at $0.01/1K input tokens, adding a 10,000-token background document costs $0.10 per request. If the document has a 30% chance of being relevant, the expected value is $0.10 × 0.3 = $0.03 relevance gain vs $0.10 cost — unprofitable. Use a tiered context strategy: (1) Always include the most relevant retrieved chunk (high expected value). (2) Include additional context only if embedding similarity exceeds 0.85 (high relevance confidence). (3) Cap total context at 8K tokens for cost control, using summarization to compress lower-ranked context. Monitor cost per relevant response to tune thresholds. This framework makes context assembly economically rational rather than maximizing context naively.

statement: LiteLLM's streaming with AWS Bedrock fails silently for models that use Bedrock's invokeModelWithResponseStream endpoint versus the converse API. LiteLLM uses different streaming implementations for Bedrock Converse API (newer, supports tool use) versus legacy invokeModel (older models). When streaming is requested for a model that is only supported via invokeModel streaming, LiteLLM may return a non-streaming response (collecting all chunks before returning) while stream=True is set. The client receives the full response as a single chunk in the iterator, which appears like streaming from the client's perspective but has full generation latency before the first token. Check Bedrock model compatibility with streaming by testing with a short max_tokens=20 request and measuring time-to-first-token.

statement: LiteLLM supports audio transcription through the unified litellm.transcription() call that routes to OpenAI Whisper, Azure Whisper, or Groq's Whisper endpoint. Usage: result = litellm.transcription(model='whisper-1', file=open('audio.mp3','rb')). Response format is normalized to OpenAI's transcription response schema: {text: str, task: str, language: str, duration: float, words: [...]}. Groq's Whisper endpoint is 10-20× cheaper than OpenAI's and faster (near real-time transcription due to LPU hardware). For applications requiring both STT (transcription) and LLM generation, LiteLLM's unified interface enables using Groq Whisper + Claude or GPT-4 in a single pipeline without managing multiple API clients.

statement: Implementing request deduplication with LiteLLM to prevent duplicate API charges from retry storms: (1) Generate a deterministic request ID: request_id = hashlib.sha256(json.dumps({'model': model, 'messages': messages}, sort_keys=True).encode()).hexdigest()[:16]. (2) Check Redis before sending: if redis.get(f'completed:{request_id}'): return cached_result. (3) Set an in-progress marker: redis.setex(f'in-progress:{request_id}', 30, '1') with 30s expiry. (4) Send request via LiteLLM. (5) On success: redis.setex(f'completed:{request_id}', 3600, json.dumps(result)). (6) On failure: delete in-progress marker to allow retry. This deduplication pattern prevents charging multiple times when network issues cause clients to retry requests that have already been processed, which is common in mobile/unreliable network environments.

statement: LiteLLM's model_list in Router requires at least one model to be available at all times — if all models enter cooldown simultaneously, new requests fail with 'All models are on cooldown'. This edge case occurs when all providers experience simultaneous outages (common during major cloud incidents) or when the cooldown_time is set too high and a brief spike causes all models to be cooled. Configure a 'last resort' model deployment with no_retry=True and high allowed_fails=100 to ensure at least one deployment never enters cooldown: this acts as a degraded fallback that always accepts requests (at potentially lower quality or higher latency) rather than failing completely. Combine with alerting on 'last resort' model usage rates to detect when primary models are systematically unavailable.

statement: Automated meeting notes with LiteLLM multi-step pipeline: (1) Audio → Whisper transcription via litellm.transcription(model='whisper-1'). (2) Transcription → speaker diarization prompt to LLM to identify speaker changes and assign roles (moderator, participants). (3) Full transcript → meeting summary with action items via litellm.completion(model='gpt-4-turbo', messages=[...], response_format={'type':'json_object'}) for structured JSON output. (4) Action items → extracted with assignee, deadline, priority. (5) Structured notes sent to Notion via API with litellm providing the intelligence layer. (6) Follow-up: litellm.completion generates email drafts for each assignee with their specific action items. Total latency for 1-hour meeting: 3-5 minutes for transcription + 45s for analysis + 15s for email drafts. Cost: approximately $0.50-2.00 per meeting depending on length and model tier.

statement: LiteLLM Proxy's admin UI (accessible at /ui when running with --ui flag) exposes all configured API keys and team budgets to any authenticated user with 'admin' role. In multi-tenant deployments where different teams should not see each other's configuration, a compromised admin token allows viewing all teams' budgets, usage patterns, and potentially guessing underlying provider key formats. LiteLLM Proxy should not be deployed with the UI enabled in production without additional authentication (reverse proxy requiring corporate SSO in front of /ui). The UI is useful for operations teams but must be access-controlled. For teams that need self-service budget management, use the API endpoints with appropriate scoping rather than the UI.

statement: LiteLLM generates model aliases (gpt-4 → gpt-4-0613, claude-3-opus → claude-3-opus-20240229) by tracking provider-announced version mappings in a centralized model_prices_and_context_window.json file maintained in the LiteLLM GitHub repository. This file is bundled with each LiteLLM release and updated with new model releases. The bundled approach ensures consistency across deployments (all instances of the same LiteLLM version use identical model metadata) but means that very recently released models may not be available until a LiteLLM version bump. For newly released models not yet in the bundled data, users can override with custom_llm_provider and manual cost configuration. The alternative (fetching model metadata from a remote source at runtime) would introduce availability risks.

statement: LiteLLM integration with Weights & Biases for ML experiment tracking: (1) Set WANDB_API_KEY environment variable. (2) Configure: litellm.success_callback=['wandb']. (3) Pass W&B run metadata: litellm.completion(..., metadata={'wandb_project':'my-project','wandb_run_id':wandb.run.id}). (4) LiteLLM logs to W&B: prompt text, response text, tokens, cost, latency as W&B Artifacts and run metrics. (5) Compare LLM call performance across experiments using W&B's run comparison view. (6) Use W&B Tables to visualize prompt/response pairs from different model variants. This integration enables treating LLM calls as first-class ML experiment artifacts alongside traditional ML metrics, enabling systematic prompt engineering and model selection workflows.

statement: LiteLLM's drop_params=True globally drops unsupported parameters for all providers, but this can silently drop intentional parameters that are supported by the target provider but not recognized as standard OpenAI parameters by LiteLLM. Example: passing thinking={'type':'enabled','budget_tokens':10000} for Claude's extended thinking feature — LiteLLM may drop this as an unrecognized parameter when drop_params=True is set globally. Fix: use drop_params=False (default) and explicitly handle provider-specific parameters. Or pass them via extra_body={'thinking':{'type':'enabled','budget_tokens':10000}} which LiteLLM always passes through verbatim regardless of drop_params. The extra_body passthrough mechanism is the correct way to use provider-specific features not yet normalized in LiteLLM.

statement: LiteLLM supports prompt caching for providers that offer it (Anthropic Claude prompt caching, Google Gemini context caching) through cache_control message blocks. For Anthropic: add {'type':'text','text':'system context here','cache_control':{'type':'ephemeral'}} to the message content array. LiteLLM normalizes this to the provider's native cache hint format. Anthropic's prompt caching reduces input token cost by 90% for cached prefixes (cached content costs $0.000003/1K vs standard $0.003/1K for Claude 3.5 Sonnet). For a 50,000-token system prompt sent 1000 times/day, prompt caching reduces cost from $150/day to $15/day. The cache prefix must remain stable for hits to occur — any change invalidates the cache. LiteLLM's tracking includes a cache_read_input_tokens field in usage statistics to report actual cache hit rates.

statement: LiteLLM's Router's latency-based routing strategy measures latency using exponential moving average (EMA) of past response times, which can be slow to respond to sudden latency degradation. If a provider suddenly increases latency from 500ms to 3000ms (common during peak hours), the EMA-weighted latency may still show 600ms (mostly historical data) for several minutes before the router starts routing away from it. This means users experience degraded latency while the router's internal model catches up. For latency-sensitive applications: use routing_strategy='usage-based-routing-v2' which tracks real-time token throughput rather than historical EMA, responding faster to load changes. Or implement a custom routing strategy with a shorter EMA window (ewm_alpha=0.5 instead of default 0.1).

statement: Document summarization at scale with LiteLLM using map-reduce: (1) Large documents split into 8K-token chunks with 200-token overlap. (2) Map phase: asyncio.gather() sends all chunks to litellm.acompletion() concurrently (one API call per chunk) with prompt 'Summarize the key points from this section in 3-5 bullet points'. (3) Rate limited to 50 concurrent requests via asyncio.Semaphore(50). (4) Reduce phase: all chunk summaries concatenated into a 'summary of summaries' prompt sent to a more capable model for final synthesis. (5) Output: structured summary with executive overview, key findings by section, recommendations. Handles documents up to 1M words (500 chunks) in 3-5 minutes on GPT-4 mini (map) + GPT-4 Turbo (reduce). Map phase uses cheap model (90% of tokens); reduce phase uses expensive model (10% of tokens).

statement: LiteLLM's retry logic does not preserve the original streaming state when retrying a failed streaming request — if a streaming request fails mid-stream (network disconnect after 50 tokens have been received), LiteLLM retries the entire request from the beginning, sending the full prompt again and starting generation from token 1. The client receives duplicate content: first the incomplete stream, then the complete retry. Applications that display streaming output must implement duplicate detection (hash comparison of received token sequences) or stop rendering before the retry completes. For critical streaming applications, implement client-side retry at the application layer rather than relying on LiteLLM's automatic retry, which restarts the stream unconditionally.

statement: Compliance document generation pipeline with LiteLLM: (1) Regulatory templates (SOC2, GDPR, HIPAA controls) stored as Jinja2 templates with required fields. (2) litellm.completion() with Claude 3 Opus generates evidence narratives for each control: system prompt includes the control requirement, company-specific context, and existing policy documents. (3) JSON output enforced with keys: control_id, requirement, evidence_narrative, gaps, recommendations. (4) Legal review queue: all generated narratives flagged confidence < 0.8 (estimated from response logprobs) go to human review. (5) Version tracking: each generation run creates a new document version with the model name and temperature recorded for audit trail. (6) Diff generation between versions: sections changed by the AI are highlighted for human review. This pipeline reduces compliance document preparation time from weeks to days.

statement: LiteLLM's temperature=0 parameter does not guarantee identical outputs when the same request is sent to the same model on the same provider. LLM providers implement temperature=0 as greedy decoding but floating-point non-determinism in GPU operations (same issue as in llama.cpp) means the same request can produce different outputs depending on server load, GPU allocation, and kernel dispatch order. LiteLLM surfaces this as 'temperature=0 means consistent but not identical outputs'. For reproducibility testing, do not rely on exact string matches; use semantic similarity (embedding cosine similarity >0.99) or factual equivalence checks. This is a property of LLM inference itself, not a LiteLLM bug — no API or framework can guarantee bit-identical outputs from temperature=0 LLM inference.

statement: LiteLLM provides a completion_cost() utility that calculates the dollar cost of a completed request: cost = litellm.completion_cost(completion_response=response). This function uses the bundled model_prices_and_context_window.json to look up input/output token costs per model and calculates: cost = (prompt_tokens × input_cost_per_token) + (completion_tokens × output_cost_per_token). The function handles: cached token discounts (Anthropic's 90% cache discount), vision token costs (image tiles), and audio token costs. For custom models not in the database, configure custom_cost via: litellm.register_model({'my-model':{'input_cost_per_token':0.000001,'output_cost_per_token':0.000002}}). Use this in callbacks to track cumulative daily spend and trigger alerts when spend exceeds budget thresholds.

statement: LiteLLM Router's model health check feature (router.set_model_health_check() or periodic pings to /health endpoint) sends real API requests to verify model availability, which costs real money and counts against rate limits. In multi-model setups with 10+ deployments, periodic health checks can consume 5-10% of the rate limit quota just for health checking. Disable automatic health checks in cost-sensitive environments and rely on the Router's passive failure detection (failed requests increment failure counter) instead. For critical infrastructure where health checks are necessary, use a dedicated low-cost model (GPT-3.5-turbo or Claude 3 Haiku) for health checks rather than the expensive models under load, and set health check interval to 60s minimum.

statement: Configuring LiteLLM for HIPAA-compliant deployments: (1) Deploy LiteLLM Proxy in a HIPAA-eligible cloud region (AWS us-east-1 with BAA, Azure East US with BAA). (2) Use only HIPAA-eligible LLM providers (Azure OpenAI with BAA, AWS Bedrock with BAA, Google Vertex AI with BAA). (3) Configure database_url to a HIPAA-eligible PostgreSQL instance with encryption at rest. (4) Enable audit logging: litellm_proxy_endpoint_logs table captures all request metadata. (5) Set up PII masking guardrail (Presidio) to strip PHI before sending to LLM API. (6) Configure network: VPC endpoints for AWS Bedrock/Azure, no public internet routing. statement: Dynamic model selection with LiteLLM based on task complexity detection: build a router that classifies request complexity before sending to LLM. Simple heuristics: question_word_count < 15 → Haiku/mini; contains 'compare' or 'analyze' → Sonnet/GPT-4-mini; contains 'code' or code block → Deepseek-Coder; contains multiple PDFs or >8K tokens input → Gemini 1.5 Pro (long context). Cost impact: routing 70% of requests to cheap models (Haiku at $0.00025/K) vs 30% to expensive (GPT-4-Turbo at $0.01/K) reduces average cost by 85% versus always-GPT-4. Quality validation: sample 5% of Haiku-routed requests and re-run through GPT-4; if 95%+ quality equivalence, routing threshold is appropriately set. Continuously tune thresholds by measuring downstream task metrics per routing tier.

statement: LiteLLM's Provider class registration for custom LLM endpoints requires exact implementation of specific abstract methods or the custom provider will silently fail with a generic 'Provider not found' error rather than a helpful missing-method error. Custom providers must implement: completion(), async_completion(), streaming_completion(), embedding() (if used), and register via litellm.custom_provider_map=[{'provider':'my-provider','custom_handler':MyProviderClass()}]. Missing any required method causes silent failure at registration time — no error is raised until that code path is hit at runtime. Test custom provider implementations comprehensively across all call types before deploying: test sync completion, async completion, streaming, and embedding separately, as each is a distinct code path.

statement: Using LiteLLM for model warm-up and connection testing at application startup: import litellm; def health_check_all_models(model_list: list[str]) -> dict: results = {}; for model in model_list: try: litellm.completion(model=model, messages=[{'role':'user','content':'ping'}], max_tokens=1); results[model]='healthy'; except Exception as e: results[model]=f'unhealthy: {str(e)}'; return results. Run this at application startup (after configuration loads but before accepting traffic) to verify all LLM providers are reachable with valid credentials. Exit with non-zero status if critical models (primary providers) are unhealthy. This prevents the common failure mode where a misconfigured API key or network policy change only manifests after the first real user request in production, instead catching it at deployment time.

statement: Financial report analysis pipeline with LiteLLM: (1) 10-K, 10-Q PDFs processed via pdfminer into text, split by section (Management Discussion, Financial Statements, Risk Factors). (2) Key metrics extracted with JSON grammar via structured output: {revenue_growth, profit_margin, debt_ratio, guidance, key_risks}. (3) Comparative analysis: send current quarter + last 4 quarters metrics to GPT-4 for trend narrative. (4) Risk flags: any risk factor containing keywords 'litigation', 'regulatory', 'going concern' sent to Claude 3 Opus for detailed analysis. (5) Output: structured JSON report with financials + natural language narrative suitable for investor briefings. statement: LiteLLM maintains a separate LiteLLM Proxy product (server) alongside the Python SDK (library) because the two serve fundamentally different deployment scenarios. The Python SDK is for applications that own their LLM routing logic in code — a startup that wants fine-grained control over fallbacks and caching in their application code. The Proxy is for organizations that want to centralize LLM governance across multiple applications without modifying each application's code — an enterprise with 20 teams where the AI platform team manages provider credentials, budgets, and safety policies centrally. The Proxy exposes an OpenAI-compatible API endpoint, enabling any OpenAI-SDK-compatible tool (without LiteLLM library dependency) to benefit from LiteLLM's routing, observability, and cost management features transparently.

statement: LiteLLM with Redis Cluster for high-availability caching in multi-region deployments: configure litellm.cache = Cache(type='redis', url='redis://redis-cluster.internal:6379', cluster_mode=True). Redis Cluster provides automatic sharding and failover — if one Redis node fails, cache operations fail gracefully (cache miss, not error) and LiteLLM sends the request to the LLM provider instead. For global deployments, configure Redis in each region and use region-specific cache instances to avoid cross-region cache access latency. Multi-region cache consistency: use cache TTL of 24-48 hours for stable content (documentation Q&A) but 5-15 minutes for dynamic content (news summaries), preventing stale cache responses from degrading user experience.

statement: LiteLLM's JSON mode with Google Gemini fails for prompts that request both JSON output and multi-turn conversation context. Gemini's JSON mode (response_mime_type='application/json') only applies to the final generation — if previous turns in the conversation contained non-JSON responses, Gemini may ignore the JSON constraint in the current turn due to pattern continuation from the conversation history. Fix: for Gemini JSON mode in multi-turn conversations, always explicitly include 'Output your response as valid JSON only, no other text' in the current user message (not just the system prompt) to override the conversational context. Alternatively, use a single-turn request for JSON extraction tasks rather than embedding them in a conversation.

statement: LiteLLM's text_completion() function (legacy completions API) works with providers that support the completion (non-chat) format, including older GPT-3.5-instruct models and some open-source model endpoints. While most modern providers favor the chat completions format, text_completion() enables using prompt-based generation styles (fill-in, continuation) where the chat format's role structure is unwanted overhead. For code generation tasks: text_completion(model='gpt-3.5-turbo-instruct', prompt='def fibonacci(n):', max_tokens=100) generates the function body starting from the prompt prefix, similar to GitHub Copilot's completion style. This is distinct from chat completions with a carefully crafted prompt — text completions use the raw prompt as a sequence prefix without any role-based instruction overhead.

statement: LiteLLM Proxy's rate limiting with Redis operates on a per-proxy-instance basis when Redis is not configured — rate limits are applied in-memory per process. With 4 proxy instances behind a load balancer, each instance enforces its own rate limit independently. A team with 100 RPM limit can effectively send 400 RPM by distributing requests across all 4 instances. For accurate rate limiting in multi-instance deployments, Redis must be configured as the shared state backend: REDIS_HOST and REDIS_PORT must be set, and the proxy must be started with --use_redis flag. Verify shared rate limiting is working: send requests from two different clients to different proxy instances and confirm they share the same rate limit counter.

statement: Handling context window exceeded errors gracefully with LiteLLM: (1) Wrap calls in try/except litellm.ContextWindowExceededError. (2) On error: call litellm.token_counter(model, messages) to get exact current token count. (3) Calculate overage: overage = token_count - litellm.get_max_tokens(model). (4) Trim conversation history: remove oldest user/assistant turns until token count is within budget. (5) Retry the call with trimmed messages. (6) Log trim events for monitoring: high trim rate indicates prompts are too long and should be redesigned. Strategy: always trim from the middle of the conversation (preserve system prompt + last 2 turns), not the beginning. Preserving the last 2 turns maintains immediate context while the system prompt maintains task framing. Implement with a while loop that removes message pairs until within budget.

statement: Automated code review pipeline with LiteLLM and GitHub Actions: (1) GitHub Action triggers on PR open/update. (2) Diff fetched via GitHub API, split into per-file changes. (3) LiteLLM routes each file to optimal model: Python/JS/TS → DeepSeek-Coder-V2 (strong code understanding), security-relevant files (auth.py, payment.py) → Claude 3.5 Sonnet (better at security analysis), documentation → GPT-4o-mini (cost-optimized). (4) Each file review structured as JSON: {issues: [{severity: 'critical'|'major'|'minor', line, description, suggestion}]}. (5) Reviews aggregated and posted as GitHub PR review comments via GitHub API. (6) Critical security issues automatically request changes; minor issues left as suggestions. This pipeline provides automated first-pass review on 100% of PRs, reducing human review time by focusing attention on flagged issues.
